examples.title: Pseudocode: Blockchain Hashing
examples.description: Demonstrates how blockchain uses hashing to create a tamper-evident chain
examples.code:
FUNCTION hash(data)
 RETURN unique_alphanumeric_string

FUNCTION create_block(data, previous_hash)
 hash_value = hash(data)
 RETURN block = {data, hash_value, previous_hash}

FUNCTION add_block(blockchain, new_block)
 IF new_block.previous_hash == blockchain.last_block.hash_value
 blockchain.add(new_block)
 ELSE
 THROW Error('Invalid previous hash')
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how each block in the blockchain contains a hash of the previous block, creating a chain. If an attacker tries to change a block, the hash will change, invalidating all subsequent blocks.

examples.title: Python: Blockchain Class
examples.description: A basic implementation of a blockchain in Python
examples.explanation: This Python code defines a `Block` class and a `Blockchain` class. The `Blockchain` class has methods to add new blocks to the chain, and each block contains a hash of the previous block.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for adding a block, O(n) for verifying the chain

examples.title: C: Blockchain Struct
examples.description: A basic implementation of a blockchain in C
examples.explanation: This C code defines a `Block` struct and a `Blockchain` struct. The `Blockchain` struct has a method to add new blocks to the chain, and each block contains a hash of the previous block.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for adding a block, O(n) for verifying the chain
